plainMeaning: This line simply names six kinds of mansion-dwelling gods of the lower world — the island-, direction-, ocean-, lightning-, thunder- and fire-youths — whose realms the worshipper is sweeping into his reverence.
recitation: dīpakumāra diśākumāra, vaḷī udadhikumāra. vidyut stanitakumāra, ane vaḷī agnikumāra.

wordByWord.gloss: the island-youth gods (Dvīpakumāra), one of the ten bhavanapati classes
wordByWord.etymology: Skt dvīpa ('island') + kumāra ('youth, prince'); the vernacular dīpa drops the conjunct of dvīpa.
wordByWord.notes: Standard cosmographic form is Dvīpakumāra; their realm lies in the islands of the lower world.

wordByWord.gloss: the direction-youth gods (Dikkumāra), governing the quarters of space
wordByWord.etymology: Skt diś / diśā ('direction, quarter') + kumāra; equivalent to the canonical Dik-kumāra.
Gujarati connective vaḷī (from Prakrit valia, Skt valita, 'turned-again'), here a list-linking 'furthermore'.

wordByWord.gloss: the ocean-youth gods (Udadhikumāra), presiding over the waters

wordByWord.etymology: Skt vidyut ('lightning', vi- + √dyut 'to flash'); the word kumāra is shared with the next term by the metre.
wordByWord.notes: Printed without the suffix; kumāra is understood, giving Vidyutkumāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Repetition of the connective vaḷī, rounding off the list.

literalTranslation: Island-youths, direction-youths, and also ocean-youths; lightning-(youths), thunder-youths, and again fire-youths.
idiomaticTranslation: And toward the Dvīpa-kumāra and Diśā-kumāra gods, and the Udadhi-kumāra too; the Vidyut- and Stanita-kumāras, and the Agni-kumāras as well.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This line is a pure roll-call: it names six of the ten classes of mansion-dwelling bhavanapati gods, each labelled by the element or sphere it governs — island (dīpa/dvīpa), the directions (diśā/dik), ocean (udadhi), lightning (vidyut), thunder (stanita) and fire (agni). The shared suffix kumāra ('ever-youthful prince') marks the whole order: these gods keep a perpetually youthful, princely form. The list runs straight on from the previous line, which had already named the Sovana (Suparṇa) and Vāyu kumāras, and it is completed by the line that follows — 'know these six abodes (e chae sthānaka): in each there are Jina-temples, seventy-six lakh, seventy-six lakh.' So the six names here are not loose decoration but the exact antecedent of that 'six': the poet enumerates the divine realms precisely so that he can next count the Jina-shrines packed into them. The order departs from the canonical Tattvārtha sequence; the poet follows metre and rhyme (the -kumāra refrain) rather than scriptural order, and lets vidyut borrow the kumāra of stanitakumāra to keep the line tight.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The bhavanapatis are the first of the four orders (nikāya) of gods and dwell in the upper layer of the Ratnaprabhā earth in the lower world. The verse's doctrinal payload is not the gods themselves but what their realms contain: śāśvata (eternal) Jina-temples and images, which even these gods venerate. Counting their dwellings is thus a step in the larger truth that reverence for the Jina is cosmic — the awakened one is honoured in every god-mansion below as much as in human shrines above — and that devas, far from being objects of worship, are themselves worshippers at eternal caityas.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: As you chant the six names, let each one open a whole region of the lower world in the mind's eye, every one of them crowded with eternal Jina-shrines. The verse trains the reciter to widen reverence past the visible temple to realms unseen, steadying right faith (samyaktva) through sheer cosmic scale rather than through any claim one could verify by counting.

notes: Verse-specific caveats: (1) દીપકુમાર is the vernacular spelling of Dvīpakumāra (the conjunct of dvīpa is simplified to dīpa) — kept verbatim. (2) વિદ્યુત્ is printed without the suffix; kumāra is understood from context and the metre, yielding Vidyutkumāra. (3) દિશાકુમાર corresponds to the canonical Dik-kumāra ('direction-youths'). (4) The order of the six classes follows the poet's metre and the -kumāra refrain, not the canonical Tattvārtha sequence. (5) The 'six' (છએ) of the following verse refers precisely to the six classes named in this line, each credited there with 76 lakh Jina-temples — a traditional devotional figure preserved as printed, not independently audited.
